key_points: Atom consists of nucleus containing protons and neutrons, and electrons revolving in discrete energy levels
Atomic number (Z) = number of protons; Mass number (A) = protons + neutrons; Number of neutrons = A – Z
Electron arrangement follows shells K, L, M, N with maximum electrons 2, 8, 18, 32
Isotopes: Atoms with same Z but different A; Isobars: Atoms with same A but different Z
Valency of an element depends on the number of electrons in outermost shell
Rutherford’s gold foil experiment showed that atom is mostly empty space with dense nucleus
Bohr’s model explains discrete energy levels; electrons can jump between levels by absorbing or emitting energy
Mass spectrometer helps determine isotopic masses and relative abundances of isotopes
examples: Carbon-12 and Carbon-14: isotopes of carbon, Z=6, A=12 & 14
Valency of oxygen (2 outer electrons needed to complete octet) = 2
formulas: Number of neutrons = Mass number – Atomic number
Mass number = Number of protons + Number of neutrons
Maximum electrons in a shell = 2n² (n = shell number)
Valency = Number of electrons required to complete octet or duet
Relative atomic mass = Σ (isotopic mass × fractional abundance of isotope)
exam_tips: Use Z and A to calculate number of protons, neutrons, and electrons
Explain isotopes and isobars with examples and calculations
Draw Bohr’s model for atoms up to 20 electrons
Calculate valency based on outermost electron configuration
Practice problems involving relative atomic mass using isotopic abundances
